title: Assembly Instructions for the Allen Institute Mouse Running Wheel System
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"tpdf2","text":"This document details the steps necessary to assemble a Variable Difficulty Upright Mouse Running Wheel with Allen Institute Headframe Clamp.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"cuyfg","text":"The reaction torque sensor is extremely sensitive and must be handled with care.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ev6t2","text":"Do not rotate either side plate more than one degree relative to the main body of the sensor— doing so will result in permanent damage.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":52},{"style":"bold","offset":118,"length":17}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fmvqj","text":"Do not apply excessive torque or force during assembly.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":38}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"n38hb","text":"While assembling, always place the sensor on a surface that allows rotation, rather than holding the main body tightly.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":25,"length":94}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"znksr","text":"The sensor has an indicator line on each side, which will be important for positioning it correctly during assembly.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":18,"length":14},{"style":"bold","offset":75,"length":24}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"wvwax","text":"When connected to the computer, the sensor value should remain around 2048 and should never exceed 2500 or drop below 1500.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":36,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ceubm","text":"Keep the torque sensor graph open and visible and ensure that the torque sensor remains connected throughout the entire assembly process.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3sgpa","text":"Always monitor the graph while operating the sensor.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

See the Onshape BOM for the latest version: \nhttps://cad.onshape.com/documents/579ff6de692121d8b6a45604","type":"unstyled"}],"entityMap":{}}}}}
steps: Safety: Use facemask and gloves
Spray 3M Super 77 adhesive on approximately 5–10 cm of one side of the foam strip.
Place the glued section of the foam strip in the surface of the wheel frame, ensuring that the edges are perfectly parallel to the frame.
Press down firmly on the glued area for 30 seconds, until it is fully adhered.
Wrap the remaining foam around the wheel frame, pulling it tightly to avoid any bumps or wrinkles.
There will be a small overlap where the ends of the foam meet. Carefully trim this overlap in small increments (about 1-2 mm at a time) until the ends are perfectly aligned with no overlap or gaps.
Spray 3M Super 77 adhesive on 5–10 cm on this other end of the foam strip.
Press it down firmly for 30 seconds, until it is fully attached.
Inspect the foam to ensure there are no bumps or wrinkles.
Let it dry for at least 30 minutes before attaching the walls
Take the 3D-printed wheel frame and a foam strip measuring 40 x 495 mm.
Tools Needed • Hand drill or drill press with M3 tap for plastic, or Tap handle (optional, for manual threading)• Clamps or a jig• Safety goggles
Safety Tips: Wear goggles Keep your hands clear of the bit.
Secure the part: Use clamps or a jig to hold the part in place. Don’t overtighten – plastic can crack.
A. Option 1 (Using a drill): Drill Settings: Use low speed (<800rpm) to avoid melting the plastic Drill the Holes: Drill slowly and steadily. Clear plastic chips by reversing the drill.
B. Option 2 (Using a tap handle – Much slower but safer): Thread manually: with light pressure, backing out periodically to clear chips.
Check the Holes: Make sure the threads are clean.
Tools Needed • Arbor press or hand press
Safety: Keep hands clear of the pressing area.
Align the Pin: Place the part on a stable surface and position the pin vertically over the hole making sure it’s not tilted.
Press the Pin: Use an arbor press or hand press to push the pin into the hole. Press slowly and steadily—no hammering and stop when the pin is fully seated.
Check the Fit: Make sure the pin is straight and firmly held in place and that there are no cracks or stress marks around the hole.
Carefully open torque sensor packaging. Place the sensor on a soft surface and connect it very carefully to the 4-wire M5 circular connector.
Connect the treadmill board:
Connect ports 1-5 of the board to the 4-wire M5 circular connector.
Connect the USB Type-C port to the computer.
On the computer:
Download the TreadmillBonsai.zip file from the Releases · AllenNeuralDynamics/Treadmill.Bonsai.Visualizer
Unzip the contents to any location on your computer.
Open the unzipped folder and doubleclick the file TorqueVisualizer.bat. This will open a Bonsai window with the treadmill workflow.
If the computer has audio output, you will hear an audio signal whose frequency depends on the torque sensor value. This allows you to detect changes in the torque easily.
Double-click the Torque node to open the real-time graph that monitors data from the torque sensor.
Start the workflow by clicking on the " Start " button.

Locate the Treadmill node in the workflow, doubleclick it, and select the COM port where the board is connected. If no other devices are connected to the computer, there should be only one available option. If there are several, you can check the correct one in the Windows Device Manager, or try each until the node changes color from gray or red to green.
Please follow the torque sensor assembly instructions provided in the Warnings tab before proceeding with the setup instructions.
creator: Daniel Alcala Alvarez
